original text: Is a continuation of Book #1---The Talking Clowns Productions Book by Christine Haymon. In the second book, you will find thirteen, five minute skits presented to me by my assistant clown director, David Holcomb, ten kid’s crusade productions of mine, and pictures of some of our facilities in the children’s department and its leaders. I wanted you to also see the difference in the young clown recruits as they approach the teenage years.

original text:
For the first time in recent memory, this Christmas’ extended family gift exchange was not a game. Gathered around the fireplace at my aunt’s house, each of her siblings chose a number to determine their turn. Then, in order, each sibling carefully took one of their mother’s precious keepsakes as their own. In a complex atmosphere of bittersweet memories, one person picked the photo of Grandma with her sisters; another selected the Christmas wreath that had hung on her door; and on it went… until nothing was left.
Just five days prior, gathered around a casket in parish pews, we prayed the Rosary in a way we never had before. Grandma’s eldest daughter, in a moment of remembrance after prayer, noted, “My mother’s favorite mystery of the Rosary was the Nativity.” Many times over the next twenty-four hours, we were reminded that Grandma prayed 15 decades of the Rosary daily, entrusting all of us to Jesus through his Heavenly Mother, but also entrusting herself.
Because of the loss of my grandmother, I re-discovered this Christmas. I noted how the Church celebrates—yes, celebrates—St. Stephen’s bloody martyrdom on December 26. On December 27, we celebrate the death of Saint John the Evangelist, who gave up each day of his life completely to God and died a natural death. Then, we celebrate the Feast of the Holy Innocents on December 28 (those innocent children whom Herod slaughtered in a paranoid rush to protect his sovereignty from a baby King). Thus, we begin the Christmas season by celebrating three martyrdoms: chosen martyrdom by blood, chosen martyrdom of daily life, and unchosen martyrdom of innocent blood.
It is a strange wonder to think that our religion celebrates death at a time like Christmas. Yet, it is not strange when I think back to the end of my grandmother’s Rosary service. Having completed the final prayer, my uncle Deacon John reflected, “We will remember always that now, whenever we pray the Rosary, we pray it along with her.” Jesus’ birthday made possible his self-gift on the Cross, transforming the curse of death into a gift: the hope of abundant and everlasting life.
When we see a body lying in a casket, it seems impossible for that person to do anything more. Death appears to be final. Yet, Christians dare to hope in knowing that God—who created that person, who knows all the hidden sufferings and trials of his or her life, and who loves perfectly—is Judge. Therefore, we must pray often in hope and trust, commending his or her soul to God who became Love Incarnate, whose ways and wisdom are far beyond our weak ways and short-sighted wisdom.
God became a human because he could not bear the thought of living without each one of us in Eternity. He was born to show us his love; penultimately expressed by his death and resurrection. That is the gift of Christmas.
To go deeper into this topic, consider watching our recent episode of Catholicism Live! We invite you to watch Catholicism Live! every Tuesday morning at 11am CST. Find out more about the show that helps you keep your faith alive at CatholicismLive.com.

original text:
Fiber-filled plums or prunes are a safe, natural way to cure constipation. Follow our easy step-by-step instructions to learn how to puree fresh plums or prunes.
Fresh plums, or prunes, are an excellent defense against constipation. A ripe plum will smell sweet at the stem and give slightly when you squeeze it; the color can vary and isn't a good indicator of ripeness. Look for fruit that's free of nicks, bruises, and other blemishes. Prunes are a great alternative when fresh plums aren't in season. One whole plum yields about four ounces of puree.
Wash the plum with a mixture of three parts water and one part white vinegar to remove bacteria. Rinse under cool running water and dry.
Bring water to a boil in a medium saucepan and then add peaches to the water for about 45 seconds. With a slotted spoon, remove plum from boiling water and plunge into an ice bath immediately. After plum has been fully submerged, remove and peel the skin with your fingers or a sharp paring knife.
Slice plum in half, lengthwise, working your way around the pit. Twist and pull the plum halves apart. Use a spoon to pry out the pit, or stick the blade of a sharp knife into the pit and twist until the pit pops out. Slice each half into even-sized slices then quarter each slice.
Puree plum in a food processor or blender until smooth. Add water as needed to reach desired consistency.
For chunkier plum puree, which is ideal for babies 10 months or older, mash the plum with a potato masher instead of pureeing it.
Cool plum puree and refrigerate leftovers in BPA-free containers for up to 3 days. Freeze leftovers for up to 3 months. Thaw overnight in your refrigerator.
If working with prunes: Cook pitted prunes until tender (about 15 minutes). Puree prunes, adding water as necessary, until smooth. Mix with foods list in step 5. Follow refrigeration and freezing instructions in step 6.

Slice plum in half, lengthwise, working your way around the pit. Twist and pull the plum halves apart. Use a spoon to pry out the pit, or stick the blade of a sharp knife into the pit and twist until the pit pops out. Slice each half into even-sized slices then quarter each slice.

Cool plum puree and refrigerate leftovers in BPA-free containers for up to 3 days. Freeze leftovers for up to 3 months. Thaw overnight in your refrigerator.
If working with prunes: Cook pitted prunes until tender (about 15 minutes). Puree prunes, adding water as necessary, until smooth. Mix with foods list in step 5. Follow refrigeration and freezing instructions in step 6.

original text:
Right now Utahns who earn between 100-400% of the federal poverty level are eligible to receive premium subsidies to purchase private insurance on healthcare.gov. However, individuals who earn below 100% FPL—even $100 below that limit—make too little to use healthcare.gov. A single person earning less than $11,490 and a family of three earning less than $19,530 can’t receive subsidies. Plus, many of them earn too much to qualify for Utah Medicaid. They are stuck in the Medicaid Coverage Gap. About 60,000 Utahns are falling into this hole.
They are teenage cancer patients.
They are residents of every legislative district in Utah.
And they are realizing that Medicaid expansion would help then live healthier and more productive lives.
Here are two stories from Utah’s Coverage Gap.
Ryan was an associate provost at a Utah college. He has a Ph.D. and over 30 years of experience in higher education administration. But when Ryan lost his job in December, he found himself, his wife, and son without affordable health insurance coverage options. Ryan knew he had to seek assistance immediately as his wife has diabetes and needs medications that cost over $1,000 a month. When he applied for coverage on healthcare.gov he was told he qualified for Medicaid. But when he applied for Medicaid through the Utah Department of Workforce Services, he was told he didn’t qualify in Utah. Ryan and his family are caught in Utah’s coverage gap. Because he is currently unemployed, and his wife is unable to work due to ongoing medical conditions, they don’t qualify for Medicaid. If Utah expands Medicaid, Ryan and his wife would have health insurance while he searches for a new job. For Ryan, health insurance means stabilizing his wife’s illness and avoiding the threat of medical bankruptcy.
David was laid off in November, 2013. He and his wife JoAnn knew that they needed insurance, so they purchased COBRA insurance. Their cost for one month of COBRA coverage was $800. Realizing they couldn’t extend coverage beyond the one month, they applied for health insurance on healthcare.gov. They were told they qualified for Medicaid in Utah. When they applied for Medicaid, they were told they don’t meet categorical requirements to be eligible.
JoAnn has Charcot Foot, a common problem for diabetics. While they had insurance under David’s previous job, she got treatment for her illness, including an amputation. JoAnn needs skin grafts and physical therapy to continue her recovery. But without insurance, JoAnn can only hope it heals by itself. David has high blood pressure, but can’t afford the medications he needs to keep it in check. Both JoAnn and David are hopeful that he will get a new job with health insurance. But if Utah closed the coverage gap, both JoAnn and David would have health insurance coverage until he finds employment.
Last week the federal government released detailed data for the first month of marketplace enrollment (October 1 through November 2). Utah’s numbers were included in the 29-page data dump.
>>A total of 6,186 Utahns have finished applications (some for more than one person), but have not yet picked a plan.
>>3,883 of the Utah applicants qualified for premium subsidies to make their insurance premiums more affordable.
>>Successful Medicaid and CHIP applicants accounted for 4,816 Utahns on the marketplace. An unknown number also applied through state agencies.
>>Utahns are waiting until the December 15 and March 31 deadlines to purchase their marketplace insurance. Here’s a comment from the 11/13/13 Salt Lake Tribune article, Fewer than 400 Utahns able to shop Obamacare exchange.
>>How many people do you know have purchased all of their Christmas presents? Face it: We are a last-minute society.
Failed Launch or Slow Start?
-If I create a new login will it work?
-How about applying at 2am?
No, that’s when the website is being overhauled.
-What if I refresh my computer screen 500 times?
Nada, and now you’ve got a repetitive stress injury. Good thing pre-existing conditions don’t matter anymore.
Two weeks after the debut of the Affordable Care Act’s marketplaces—including healthcare.gov here in Utah–the promise of reform has been temporarily replaced by an error message.
But it will get fixed.
Yes, some Utahns have signed up for new insurance. If it wasn’t for the success of Phil Sherburne, Leia Bell and their three kids, we’d all probably be at stage four: “Depression.” Soon after October 1st Phil signed up his family for a silver-level insurance policy that cost them $123 a month. And it took him “about an hour,” he told the Salt Lake Tribune. How? We don’t know. As we drafted this article our healthcare.gov login screen was stuck in digital purgatory.
Criticism of healthcare.gov’s abortive launch is blunt and universal.
Ezra Klein called the debut a “failure” in the Washington Post’s Wonkblog. The New York Times quoted an insurance official describing the federal website as “awful, just awful.” And Avik Roy, never a fan of Obamacare, headlined a recent a Forbes.com post with “third-world experience,” referring to a now prescient March 2013 warning from the chief information officer at the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS).
Still, the middle of October is 14 days into a 180-day open enrollment period. Even if you count February 15th as the drop-dead enrollment date to avoid a tax penalty, about 90 percent of the enrollment window remains.
And so the question isn’t whether healthcare.gov will be fixed.
The question is… when it is finally fixed; will the millions of people who have struggled to log on notice that the website’s door is now open?
Perhaps yes, suggests this opinion piece in the Times. “Interested customers will come back often,” it explains, citing an average of 18 inquiries on the 2006-era Massachusetts Health Connector website before a person signed up for coverage.
The Obama administration has set a goal of seven million Americans signed up for new coverage in 2014. And for the marketplaces to maintain the proper risk balance, many of the new enrollees must be the “young invincibles” for whom health insurance isn’t a medical necessity, but convenient financial security or something that makes mom happy.
So far the state-run exchanges are doing better than the federal exchanges—including 1,000 applicants a day in Kentucky, and 305 newly covered lives during the first week of Colorado’s marketplace. Where is Utah? Besides the Sherburne-Bell family—we don’t know. This Advisory Board post estimates 5,000 successful applicants among the 26 states with federal marketplaces—compared to almost 25,000 in the state-run exchanges. As of mid-October, Utah data is non-existent.
For advocates, enrollment specialists, and health professionals, the fallback position for now is education and outreach.
We can explain what healthcare.gov will do even if the reality isn’t available yet in Utah. Plus, we can promote helpful workarounds like the Kaiser Family Foundation’s subsidy calculator—and the new plan preview tool at healthcare.gov—which doesn’t require a login or identity verification.
Plus, UHPP’s calendar of “Health Reform 101” presentations is staying active.
We’ve held 67 events since January, reaching almost 2,900 Utahns. And we have 15 more educational events scheduled for the second half of October, including presentations in Provo, Orem, Ogden, and Moab. To schedule a “just the facts” educational presentation about the ACA for your group or organization, email Jason Stevenson, or call him 801-433-2299, x5.
Utah’s decision on whether to expand Medicaid coverage is one of the last pieces of the health reform puzzle to fall into place. And thanks to recent comments and events, we now have a likely timeline for when a decision will be made.
A community workgroup, organized by the Utah Department of Health, has been studying Medicaid expansion throughout the summer. To cover more ground, the workgroup split into five subgroups each focused on a specific approach: Full expansion; Partial expansion; Block grant; Full-family coverage; and Charity care. UHPP has been active in all subgroups, and Matt Slonaker co-chairs the Full expansion group. This recent Salt Lake Tribune article describes the self-reported pluses and minuses of each approach to expansion.
Next up: After the full community workgroup meets again on September 5th, each subgroup will report during Governor Gary Herbert’s Health Summit on September 26th. Then Gov. Hebert will decide in December whether to include funding for Medicaid expansion in his next budget proposal. If he proposes some kind of Medicaid expansion, the governor still needs the approval of the Utah legislature during the 2014 legislative session. According to a state-sponsored study, 123,000 Utahns (including these folks) would become eligible for coverage if Utah expands Medicaid.
On August 1st the community workgroup met to hear to preliminary reports from each of the five subgroups. This event also served as a dress rehearsal for the governor’s health summit in September.
UHPP observed two major developments during the August 1st meeting. First, Dr. Sven Wilson, a BYU economist who earned a PhD at the University of Chicago, told the workgroup that Medicaid expansion is a good, lean investment for the state. Dr. Wilson also shared his calculation that expanding Medicaid would cost Utah around $158 per new enrollee per year (see page 4). Second, consensus is building across multiple subgroups to employ premium subsidies as an alternative to the traditional Medicaid expansion (see more below). From UHPP’s perspective, the August 1st meeting was productive and educational for the workgroup members and the general public. However, one idea did not receive enough attention: The notion that federal government will only fund a plan that covers Utah’s entire Medicaid expansion population.
Most of the August 1st meeting was taken up with presentations by the five subgroups. Each subgroup had at least one plan and the Block Grant group, headed by Sen. Todd Weiler (R-Woods Cross) discussed three proposals. Some presentations were substantive, and others were more conceptual. But one thing was evident after hearing from all the groups: Progress is being made as Utah’s policymakers and lawmakers focus on studying the issue and developing real solutions.
One theme shared by several subgroups was the use of premium subsidies to enable low-income Utahns to purchase private insurance instead of get Medicaid. This approach would leverage new federal funds to help Medicaid beneficiaries purchase private coverage through a state’s new health insurance exchanges. Arkansas pioneered this approach, and states like Ohio, Tennessee, Florida, and Pennsylvania might follow its lead.
Three subgroups (Full expansion, Partial expansion, and Block Grant) are considering subsidy-based alternatives to the Medicaid expansion. The good news is that, if done right, a subsidy-based plan may be eligible for the federal match that starts at 100% and phases down to 90% by 2020.
Unfortunately, in UHPP’s estimation, not enough attention was paid to whether the various premium-subsidy proposals would be eligible for the 100% to 90% federal match. Without the full match, Utah would be stuck with the same finite resources of the current 70% federal match rate. Plus, the federal government has made it clear that partial expansion plans won’t receive the full match rate. Going forward, UHPP will work to determine whether Utah’s various premium-subsidy proposals will be eligible for the full federal match.
Several meetings in August and September will lay the groundwork for the state’s upcoming decision on Medicaid expansion.
Note: During this meeting the committee will discuss the model for charity care outlined in the 8/16/13 document “Strengthening Our Safety Net: A Proposal.” This proposal would recommend that Utah charter a nonprofit to establish eight outpatient clinics along the Wasatch Front where patients could receive free care. Three clinics would be located in Salt Lake county, two in Utah county, and one each in Davis, Weber, and Cache counties.
According to recent remarks by Gov. Herbert, his decision on the Medicaid expansion will be reflected in his budget proposal in December.
Last Wednesday HHS released the final rules on Essential Health Benefits (EHB)—the “floor” of ten benefits (like hospitalization, maternity and newborn care, and mental health services) that must be offered by insurance plans on each state’s individual and small business exchanges, Medicaid state plans, and on any new insurance plans offered outside the exchanges. The rule also prohibits discrimination based on age or pre-existing conditions, describes prescription drug benefits and determines levels of coverage. For more details on what changed between the proposed rules unveiled last November and the update last week, check out this excellent summary from Georgetown’s Center for Children and Families.
Utah did not change or upgrade its EHB standard during the comment period.
See Utah’s current EHB standard here: As you can see, there are not a lot of frills in Utah’s standard, though the state seems to meet the minimum requirements. For example, that standard still covers only eight outpatient mental health visits per year.
But Nehring says the tacit approval given by HHS to Utah’s benchmark plan seems to indicate that they either didn’t notice that Utah’s age limitations for children’s dental and vision care in violation of the Affordable Care Act standards, or they didn’t care.
Next Step: UHPP and partners will take up these issues directly with CCIIO (the agency that oversees exchanges) in our conference call with the head of CCIIO, Gary Cohen, and his staff on February 27th.
>>Check out the final rule on Essential Health Benefits.

Next up: After the full community workgroup meets again on September 5th, each subgroup will report during Governor Gary Herbert's Health Summit on September 26th. Then Gov. Hebert will decide in December whether to include funding for Medicaid expansion in his next budget proposal. If he proposes some kind of Medicaid expansion, the governor still needs the approval of the Utah legislature during the 2014 legislative session. According to a state-sponsored study, 123,000 Utahns (including these folks) would become eligible for coverage if Utah expands Medicaid.
On August 1st the community workgroup met to hear to preliminary reports from each of the five subgroups. This event also served as a dress rehearsal for the governor's health summit in September.
UHPP observed two major developments during the August 1st meeting. First, Dr. Sven Wilson, a BYU economist who earned a PhD at the University of Chicago, told the workgroup that Medicaid expansion is a good, lean investment for the state. Dr. Wilson also shared his calculation that expanding Medicaid would cost Utah around $158 per new enrollee per year (see page 4). Second, consensus is building across multiple subgroups to employ premium subsidies as an alternative to the traditional Medicaid expansion (see more below). From UHPP's perspective, the August 1st meeting was productive and educational for the workgroup members and the general public. However, one idea did not receive enough attention: The notion that federal government will only fund a plan that covers Utah's entire Medicaid expansion population.
Most of the August 1st meeting was taken up with presentations by the five subgroups. Each subgroup had at least one plan and the Block Grant group, headed by Sen. Todd Weiler (R-Woods Cross) discussed three proposals. Some presentations were substantive, and others were more conceptual. But one thing was evident after hearing from all the groups: Progress is being made as Utah's policymakers and lawmakers focus on studying the issue and developing real solutions.
One theme shared by several subgroups was the use of premium subsidies to enable low-income Utahns to purchase private insurance instead of get Medicaid. This approach would leverage new federal funds to help Medicaid beneficiaries purchase private coverage through a state's new health insurance exchanges. Arkansas pioneered this approach, and states like Ohio, Tennessee, Florida, and Pennsylvania might follow its lead.
Three subgroups (Full expansion, Partial expansion, and Block Grant) are considering subsidy-based alternatives to the Medicaid expansion. The good news is that, if done right, a subsidy-based plan may be eligible for the federal match that starts at 100% and phases down to 90% by 2020.
Unfortunately, in UHPP's estimation, not enough attention was paid to whether the various premium-subsidy proposals would be eligible for the 100% to 90% federal match. Without the full match, Utah would be stuck with the same finite resources of the current 70% federal match rate. Plus, the federal government has made it clear that partial expansion plans won't receive the full match rate. Going forward, UHPP will work to determine whether Utah's various premium-subsidy proposals will be eligible for the full federal match.
Several meetings in August and September will lay the groundwork for the state's upcoming decision on Medicaid expansion.
Note: During this meeting the committee will discuss the model for charity care outlined in the 8/16/13 document "Strengthening Our Safety Net: A Proposal." This proposal would recommend that Utah charter a nonprofit to establish eight outpatient clinics along the Wasatch Front where patients could receive free care. Three clinics would be located in Salt Lake county, two in Utah county, and one each in Davis, Weber, and Cache counties.
According to recent remarks by Gov. Herbert, his decision on the Medicaid expansion will be reflected in his budget proposal in December.
Last Wednesday HHS released the final rules on Essential Health Benefits (EHB)—the "floor" of ten benefits (like hospitalization, maternity and newborn care, and mental health services) that must be offered by insurance plans on each state's individual and small business exchanges, Medicaid state plans, and on any new insurance plans offered outside the exchanges. The rule also prohibits discrimination based on age or pre-existing conditions, describes prescription drug benefits and determines levels of coverage. For more details on what changed between the proposed rules unveiled last November and the update last week, check out this excellent summary from Georgetown's Center for Children and Families.
Utah did not change or upgrade its EHB standard during the comment period.
See Utah's current EHB standard here: As you can see, there are not a lot of frills in Utah's standard, though the state seems to meet the minimum requirements. For example, that standard still covers only eight outpatient mental health visits per year.
But Nehring says the tacit approval given by HHS to Utah's benchmark plan seems to indicate that they either didn't notice that Utah's age limitations for children's dental and vision care in violation of the Affordable Care Act standards, or they didn't care.
Next Step: UHPP and partners will take up these issues directly with CCIIO (the agency that oversees exchanges) in our conference call with the head of CCIIO, Gary Cohen, and his staff on February 27th.
>>Check out the final rule on Essential Health Benefits.

Howlite was first discovered in Nova Scotia in 1868 by Canadian mineralogist, Henry How. It is most commonly found in Canada and the U.S., but it has also been found in Mexico, Germany, Russia, Turkey, Pakistan, and Namibia. It’s raw form grows in cauliflower-like masses. Natural Howlite is an opaque white with grey veining, similar to the beautiful white & gray marble counter tops everyone wants in their kitchens right now. It has often been dyed in an attempt to pass it off as other, more expensive gemstones (typically turquoise or coral), but recently it has been easier to find in its natural white form. We’re so happy about that because it is beautiful in it’s natural state too.
A beneficial stone for pretty much everyone, Howlite is calming and soothes emotions. It brings a level of peace, happiness, and contentment to the wearer. The calming nature of Howlite reduces anxiety, tension, stress, pain, and anger. It can also increase awareness, wisdom, and enlightenment. In addition to all of that, Howlite can be used to combat insomnia and quiet overactive minds.
Stop into Bead World to feel the calming, peaceful vibes of Howlite.

original text:
“I do not like carving the world into segments; we are one world”, said the only lady Prime Minister of India, Indira Gandhi. Daughter of the nation’s very first Prime Minister, Jawaharlal Nehru, Indira Gandhi was one of the longest serving Prime Minister of the country with a reign of around 15 years.
Known for her remarkable speeches and strong views on various social and political issues, Indira Gandhi often took the crowd by storm with her extraordinary presence. Outspoken, yet humble, she had much to contribute to the nation.
On her 34th death anniversary, to pay tribute to this powerful leader, we bring to you a collection of some inspirational sayings by Indira Gandhi on knowledge, freedom, power, and feminism.
* Happiness is a state of mind, you know. I don’t think you are permanently happy. One is happy about certain things and not so happy about others.
* To be liberated, a woman must feel free to be herself, not in rivalry to man but in the context of her own capacity and her personality.
* Popularity is not a guarantee of quality.
* The greatest of all contraceptives is affluence.
* You soon realize that the peak you’ve climbed was one of the lowest, that the mountain was part of a chain of mountains, that there are still so many, so many mountains to climb…And the more you climb, the more you want to climb – even though you’re dead tired.
* The purpose of life is to believe, to hope, and to strive.
* You must learn to be still in the midst of activity and vibrantly alive in repose.
* Difficulties can’t be eliminated from life. Individuals will always have them, countries will always have them…The only thing is to accept them, if possible overcome them, otherwise to come to terms with them. It’s all right to fight, yes, but only when it’s possible”.
* Whenever you take a step forward, you are bound to disturb something.

* Popularity is not a guarantee of quality.
* The greatest of all contraceptives is affluence.
* You soon realize that the peak you've climbed was one of the lowest, that the mountain was part of a chain of mountains, that there are still so many, so many mountains to climb...And the more you climb, the more you want to climb – even though you're dead tired.
* The purpose of life is to believe, to hope, and to strive.
* You must learn to be still in the midst of activity and vibrantly alive in repose.
* Difficulties can't be eliminated from life. Individuals will always have them, countries will always have them...The only thing is to accept them, if possible overcome them, otherwise to come to terms with them. It's all right to fight, yes, but only when it's possible".
* Whenever you take a step forward, you are bound to disturb something.

It’s an exciting time in the Gossipverse: this tuesday (my b-day!) the season 2 DVDs come out and my book is already shipping from the Amazons (.com and .ca).
And we’ve booked a date for the launch party of my book: Monday September 14! I’ll post the details soon, but the general game plan: cocktails & snacks & general merriment followed by the premiere of season 3! So save the date, Torontonians.
Talking Gossip After Dark Podcast!

original text:
The rise of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and automation is affecting people's lives and customer-service experiences, and it is also starting to have a big impact on contact center operations, including intraday management and monitoring. Yet what is the real difference between AI and automation and which will develop and improve intraday opportunities the most?
Join three of Teleopti’s product experts as they explore the future of Intraday Automation and AI, for Teleopti WFM and in general, as well as what this means for the individual, whether the planner, manager or agent. How can WFM Intraday Management incorporate automation and AI to create a balanced environment of control and flexibility?

Join three of Teleopti's product experts as they explore the future of Intraday Automation and AI, for Teleopti WFM and in general, as well as what this means for the individual, whether the planner, manager or agent. How can WFM Intraday Management incorporate automation and AI to create a balanced environment of control and flexibility?

original text:
Mario M. Leitao Jr, MD, FACOG, FACS, fellowship director, gynecology service, director, Minimal Access and Robotic Surgery Program, Department of Surgery, Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, discusses patient eligibility for neoadjuvant chemotherapy in advanced ovarian cancer.
For patients with newly diagnosed advanced ovarian cancer, primary debulking surgery is still the recommended approach, says Leitao. However, for patients who are ineligible to undergo surgery, neoadjuvant chemotherapy may be an option. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy is typically considered in patients older than the age of 70, those with widespread metastatic disease, and those with a suboptimal performance status or low albumin level, says Leitao. Medical fitness can also help determine if a patient is able to undergo the extensive surgery.
If patients do not meet all 3 of these criteria, a CT scan is ordered to examine the patient’s disease distribution. This can help determine whether or not a complete cytoreduction is possible, and whether neoadjuvant chemotherapy should be given first, says Leitao.
